attributes.stem:
A 63-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a one-week history of dull upper abdominal discomfort. He has no other symptoms. He had gallstone-related pancreatitis three months ago, followed by an uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy two months ago. He has no other significant medical history. He does not take any regular medications. He does not smoke and drinks about two glasses of wine on weekends. There has been no recent travel.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 74/min, blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Abdominal examination reveals mild epigastric tenderness without guarding or masses. There is no scleral icterus or peripheral stigmata of chronic liver disease. His blood results show:

  • Lipase 60 U/L (10–70)

  • Bilirubin 9 μmol/L (3–20)

  • Alanine aminotransferase 24 U/L (5–40)

  • Alkaline phosphatase 92 U/L (30–120)

  • C-reactive protein 3 mg/L (<5).

A CT scan is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
options: Reassurance and review in one month
attributes.explanation:
The CT scan shows a 7 cm well-circumscribed, homogenous, low-attenuation peripancreatic collection abutting the posterior wall of the stomach, with a thin, smooth wall and no gas locules (blue triangle in image below). Supported by the history of a recent episode of pancreatitis, this has all the features of a pancreatic pseudocyst.

Pancreatic pseudocysts are encapsulated collections of enzyme-rich fluid that develop after pancreatitis. Many resolve without intervention over several weeks if there are no complications. Decisions about intervention should be based on symptoms, complications (infection, bleeding, rupture), obstruction, or failure to resolve after an adequate period of observation with evidence of wall maturation on imaging. A size threshold alone does not mandate drainage.

Reassurance and review in one month is appropriate because this person is well, afebrile, and has only mild discomfort without features of infection, gastric outlet or biliary obstruction, or bleeding. The collection is consistent with a mature pseudocyst after recent pancreatitis, and most such collections resolve or improve with watchful waiting. Short-interval clinical review with repeat imaging if symptoms persist balances safety and avoids unnecessary procedures.

Magnetic resonance imaging scan is unnecessary as the contrast-enhanced computed tomography already characterises a simple, homogeneous, thin-walled collection without solid debris or complications. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography can be helpful when delineating ductal anatomy or walled-off necrosis, but additional imaging is unlikely to change management in this stable, minimally symptomatic person.

Percutaneous aspiration is not indicated due to the high risk of external pancreatic fistula and recurrence. Percutaneous catheter drainage is generally reserved for infected collections not amenable to endoscopic routes or when the collection does not communicate with the gastrointestinal lumen.

Endoscopic cyst-gastrostomy is preferred when intervention is required for symptomatic or complicated collections that are mature and bulge into the stomach or duodenum. In this case, there are no alarm symptoms, no infection, and no obstructive features; therefore, endoscopic drainage would be premature.

Laparoscopy and drainage is more invasive and is now uncommon as a first-line approach, reserved for cases where endoscopic routes are not feasible or have failed, or where there are surgical complications. It is not justified in a stable person with mild symptoms and no complications.
learningPoints: Most pancreatic pseudocysts after acute pancreatitis resolve spontaneously over 6–8 weeks if asymptomatic and without complications.
Intervention for a pancreatic pseudocyst is reserved for persistent symptoms, infection, bleeding, obstruction, or failure to resolve after an appropriate interval.
Endoscopic internal drainage is preferred when intervention is needed and the collection is mature, adherent to the stomach or duodenum, and symptomatic.
references: Matsuoka L, Alexopoulos SP. Surgical Management of Pancreatic Pseudocysts. Gastrointest Endoscopy Clin N Am 28 (2018) 131–141 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.giec.2017.11.002
Andalib I, Dawid E, Kahaleh M. Modern Management of Pancreatic Fluid Collections. J Clin Gastroenterol 2018; 52:97–104
Gaillard F, Walizai T, Bell D, et al. Pancreatic pseudocyst. Reference article, Radiopaedia.org (Accessed on 25 Aug 2025) https://doi.org/10.53347/rID-6938
